transcript:
Right now you're watching light move through a tangle of pale threads in space — but notice it never starts empty. The very first frame already has twelve hundred points of light mid-journey, some of them aged decades into lives you never saw begin.

Here's the thing that got me: in the setup, there's a function literally called "arrival without origin," and it doesn't generate a path — it calls "select groove by traffic" to choose from curves that already exist, named "grooves preceding me." The particle doesn't carve anything; it just lights up a channel that was there first. And every eight seconds or so, "redistribute traffic" quietly reshuffles which channels get busier — a pattern shift the piece itself didn't choose, described in a comment as "pattern I did not author."

So what you're seeing isn't creation, it's inheritance — light discovering the shape of paths worn by traffic before the piece ever turned on. The particles aren't the story. The grooves are.
